title: A tunable feedback-controlled magnetic trap for a magnet in free fall
authors: Changhao Xu, Alexander Heidt, Mohammadreza Nematollahi, Christoph Lotz, Ernst Maria Rasel, Yan Liu, Wei Ji, Dmitry Budker
abstract: Ferromagnets in free space are predicted to exhibit pure Larmor precession at near-zero magnetic fields and provide exceptional sensitivity for magnetometry and gyroscopy. Notably, pure Larmor precession has not been observed in a macroscopic ferromagnetic particle, despite its fundamental importance and potential for probing relativistic effects and dark-matter interactions. Realizing such dynamics requires true free fall to eliminate clamping losses and trap-induced systematics. A central challenge is designing a tunable trap that is weak enough to permit near-free evolution yet robust enough to withstand the disturbances of launch and release. Here, we propose and demonstrate a novel master proportional-integral-differential magnetic trap (MPIDMT) combining a PID-controlled coil system with a master control coil system. Implemented in the third-generation drop tower - Einstein-Elevator, during the microgravity phase the system stably levitates a ferromagnetic particle against shock accelerations up to 1.5 g and resolves its motion in both a low-field (0.4 g) configuration and in pure free fall. These results represent a key step toward free-fall ferromagnetic magnetometry, the long-sought direct observation of macroscopic Larmor precession, and future space-based experiments.
summary: This paper presents the development and experimental testing of a novel Master Proportional-Integral-Differential Magnetic Trap (MPIDMT) designed to trap ferromagnetic particles in near-free-fall conditions. By using a master bias coil, the trap overcomes the inherent instability of conventional systems in microgravity. The successful demonstration allows for precise measurement of magnet dynamics, moving closer to observing fundamental phenomena like macroscopic Larmor precession in space.
main_problem: The primary goal is to observe pure Larmor precession of a macroscopic ferromagnetic particle in true free fall, which requires overcoming systematic errors from magnetic traps.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated stable levitation and controlled motion of the magnet in the microgravity phase of a drop tower, achieving stable trapping down to a low bias field of 0.4 g.
method: They developed and tested a novel Master Proportional-Integral-Differential Magnetic Trap (MPIDMT) system, combining PID control with a master bias coil.
model_or_system: The system uses a PID-controlled slave coil system biased by a master coil to maintain stable levitation. The experiment is performed on the Einstein-Elevator drop tower, allowing testing across various gravity regimes, including near-free fall.
key_observables: Position and velocity distributions of the magnet during low-field trapping and free-fall periods.
important_parameters: Stable operation against shock accelerations up to 1.5 g; stable trapping demonstrated at a low bias field of 0.4 g.
main_assumptions: The PID model is a first-order linearized approximation, and the quality of free fall is limited by residual acceleration from the drop tower platform.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the experimental setup in the Einstein-Elevator, the control scheme using a QPD, and velocity profiles showing residual motion after current cutoff in both ambient and vacuum conditions.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the physical challenge (Larmor precession in free fall), proposes the MPIDMT solution, details the experimental implementation and stability analysis using the drop tower, and presents results showing stable operation and controlled release.
why_interesting: The work directly addresses the control and measurement of magnetic dynamics in near-zero gravity, which is crucial for quantum sensing applications involving magnetic moments, linking classical control theory to fundamental quantum physics measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental setup in the Einstein-Elevator . (a) Schematic of the setup with the master-PID magnetic trap (MPIDMT). A mag- net is levitated between an upper slave coil and a lower master coil. The quadrant photodetector (QPD) is for position feedback. (b) Top: a photograph of the experimental setup inside the Einstein-Elevator. The optical and PID control units and the silver-colored cylindrical magnetic shield are mounted on the carrier and are shown at the cen- ter. Bottom: side view of the the levitated magnet illuminated with a laser beam.

FIG. 2. Stability analysis under different equivalent bias accelera- tions. Both the applied disturbance and the bias field are expressed in units of g ≈9.8m/s2. When the shock-acceleration amplitude re- mains below the threshold—i.e., within the shaded region—the mag- net stays stably trapped.

FIG. 3. Programmed sequence control on MPIDMT and the se- quence from Einstein-Elevator to be synchronized. The lower panel shows the programmed profile of the EE, including the launch (accel- eration), free fall, and deceleration phases. The upper trace displays the voltages for the master (blue) and slave (orange) coils, alongside the QPD measured voltage (green), marking the critical events: re- ceipt of the trigger signal at time t1, activation of PID control at t2, reduction of the bias field at t3, and cutoff of all currents at t4. The QPD-signal fluctuations during the EE launch and deceleration result from vibration-induced noise in the optical system. During t3 to t4 in...

FIG. 4. Probability distributions of the (a) position and (b) velocity of the magnet during the trapped intervals t3 and t4. Both distribu- tions are well described by Gaussian functions. The 90% confidence intervals correspond to spatial and velocity spreads of 4.9 µm and 1.9 mm/s, respectively.

FIG. 5. Velocity profile— (a) Plot obtained by averaging different flights with ambient pressure in the gondola. The current cut off hap- pens at t0 = 0.2s (t4 in Fig. 3), a mean residual velocity of 0.6mm/s at the cut off moment mainly comes from oscillation inside the mag- netic trap. A clear residual acceleration upward is observed, with a mean value of 0.098m/s2. (b) Velocity profile after releasing the magnet for flight under a vacuum of 10 mbar between pressure hull and the gondola. The current is switched off at the same moment, t0 = 0.2s (t4 in Fig. 3).
